title: Keyboard shortcut vs command palette vs menu menubar vs toolbar vs undo vs redo
summary: Use a keyboard shortcut for a direct key combination that invokes one known command. Use command palette for searchable command discovery, menu menubar for persistent command grouping, toolbar for visible view-scoped controls, and undo or redo for edit-history semantics.
decisionRules: Choose keyboard shortcut when one memorized key combination directly invokes one command, focuses one surface, or toggles one mode without opening a command browser.
Choose command palette when users need searchable command discovery through search, ranking, filtering, or browsing many commands before executing one result with clear scope.
Choose menu menubar when commands need stable visible grouping, submenu ownership, disabled or checked menu item state, and desktop-style arrow navigation.
Choose toolbar when repeated view-scoped controls should stay visible beside the editor, canvas, table, media view, or selected object they affect.
Choose undo when the design problem is reversing the most recent operation and preserving edit-history semantics across visible controls and shortcuts.
Choose redo when the design problem is reapplying an undone operation while edit-history semantics and the history branch are still valid.

Keyboard shortcut must define command name, key chord, platform variant, scope, conflict rule, visible hint, aria-keyshortcuts exposure where appropriate, enabled state, disabled reason, confirmation or undo for risky commands, and a remapping or disable path for character-key shortcuts.
Keyboard shortcuts must not replace visible commands, navigation, menus, buttons, or focus traversal for primary workflows; they accelerate paths that remain available by other means.
Do not bind single-character shortcuts globally unless users can turn them off, remap them, or use them only while a relevant component has focus.
Resolve shortcut conflicts against browser, operating-system, screen reader, text editing, form field, command palette, menubar, toolbar, undo, redo, and product-level command maps before shipping.
failureModes: A hidden shortcut is the only way to save, submit, search, delete, or navigate to an important area.
Control+S is shown on macOS instead of Command+S, or platform variants are missing from shortcut help.
A single-letter global shortcut fires while a speech input user dictates or while a text field is active.
The shortcut hint says Delete but the key command archives, moves, or triggers an irreversible side effect.
Disabled shortcuts still fire because the keyboard handler ignores current command availability.
The command palette, toolbar, and menu show different shortcuts for the same command.
A risky shortcut runs without confirmation, undo, review, or a status message naming the affected object.

sources.note: Supports turn off, remap, or focus-scope requirements for character-key shortcuts.
Supports exposing implemented shortcuts to assistive technology.
Supports platform-specific shortcut conventions and common text-editing shortcuts.
Supports product command identifiers, platform keybindings, and command availability in an expert tool.
